content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 28, 1994

Renee M. Westa-Lusk
Chairwoman
34th Assembly District
Democratic Committee
405 Montefino
Ridgecrest, CA 93555

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-94-309

Dear Ms. Westa-Lusk:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the responsibilities of the 34th Assembly District
Democratic Committee under the provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Since your advice request is general in nature,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Is the 34th Assembly District Democratic Committee a
"recipient" committee?

a. What is the function of a recipient committee?

_______________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-309
Page 2


     b.  What is the 34th Assembly District Democratic
Committee permitted to do with funds raised?

     2.  Does the Act limit the 34th Assembly District Democratic
Committee's use of funds to contribute to candidates?

     3.  Does the Act limit which campaigns the 34th Assembly
District Democratic Committee may contribute to or require the
committee to contribute to all other campaigns affecting the
Assembly District?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  According to the Statement of Organization filed for the
34th Assembly District Democratic Committee, the committee is a
"recipient" committee.

     a and b.  Recipient committees may raise funds and make
contributions to candidates for office.

     2.  The Act does not prohibit the 34th Assembly District
Democratic Committee from making contributions to candidates.
However, contribution limits do exist with respect to special
elections.  In addition, local jurisdictions may limit
contributions.

     3.  Discretion as to whom to contribute to is left with the
34th Assembly District Democratic Committee.  No provision in the
Act mandates that a contribution be made.  No provision of the Act
requires that a committee contribute to all candidates in a given
election when the committee wishes to contribute to only select
committees in that election.

                                FACTS

     You are the chairwoman for the 34th Assembly District
Democratic Committee (ADDC), which is chartered by the Democratic
Party.  You have asked whether the Act limits the ability of the
34th Assembly District Democratic Committee to participate in
campaigns.

                              ANALYSIS

Recipient Committees

     According to the Statement of Organization filed for the 34th
Assembly District Democratic Committee, the committee is a
"recipient" committee.  A "recipient committee" is any person or
combination of persons who directly or indirectly receives
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
(Section 82030(a).)

Our File No. I-94-309
Page 3


     Section 84101 requires that all recipient committees file a
Statement of Organization, Form 410, with the Office of the
Secretary of State within 10 days of qualification under
subdivision (a) of Section 82013. (Section 84101.) The Statement
of Organization includes a statement of purpose which provides a
"...a brief description of [the committee's] political activities,
including whether it supports or opposes candidates or measures
and whether such candidates or measures have common
characteristics such as political party affiliation." (Section
84102(d).)

     We note that on the Statement of Organization for the ADDC,
it was stated that the purpose of the committee was to "select and
elect democratic candidates, register voters, encourage voters to
vote, and raise funds to carry out these activities." Please be
aware that the description on the Statement of Organization does
not prohibit activity not listed. However, substantial changes in
the activities or structure of the committee, such as a change of treasurer
or of the name of the
committee, require filing an amendment to the Statement of
Organization within 10 days of the change.³

     However, the Commission has determined that if the Statement
of Organization outlines the general activities of a committee and
subsequent political activity will not change the original
stated, no amendment need be made. (Baehr Advice
purposes as
Letter, A-77-329; Haddad Advice Letter, No. A-87-170.) Making
contributions to candidates would appear to fit in the purpose
described on the ADDC's Statement of Organization.

Permissible Use of Funds

     Contributions accepted by the ADDC are considered to be
"campaign funds" subject to restrictions on their use. (Section
89511(a).) The general rule for the use of campaign funds is that
any expenditure must be, at a minimum, reasonably related to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.)
Where an expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on the
candidate or other controlling person, the expenditure must be
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.⁴ (Section 89512.)


___________________________
     ³ The amendment is required within 24 hours if specified changes
occur within 16 days preceding an election in which a recipient
committee is required to file preelection statements pursuant to
Sections 84200.7 or 84200.8. (Section 84103.)

     ⁴ A "substantial personal benefit" is defined as a direct
personal benefit with a value of more than $100 to a candidate,
elected officer, or any individual or individuals with authority
to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a committee.
(Section 89511(b)(3); Regulation 18580.)

Our File No. I-94-309
Page 4


Contributions to Candidates

     Thus, so long as an expenditure is for a political purpose
and does not confer a substantial personal benefit on any person
involved in the decisionmaking process of the committee, the
expenditure is permitted.  Making contributions to candidates or
committees is related to a political purpose and is permissible
under the Act.  (Hertzberg Advice Letter, No. A-94-109; Keene
Advice Letter, No. I-93-409; Speers Advice Letter, No. I-92-567.)

Contribution Limits

     Section 85305 only limits contributions to state and local
candidates in special elections and special runoff elections.5
Section 85305 provides that no person may make, and no candidate
for elective office, or campaign treasurer, shall solicit or
accept any contribution or loan for a special election or special
runoff election which would cause the amount contributed or loaned
to exceed the contribution limitations which apply to the
contributor for the particular election cycle.

     The "special election cycle" means the day on which the
office becomes vacant until the day of the special election.
(Section 85305(b)(1).)  The "special runoff election cycle" means
the day after the special election until the day of the special
runoff election.6  (Section 85305(b)(2).)

     1.  Broad Based Political Committee (a committee of persons
which has been in existence for more than six months, receives
contributions from one hundred or more persons, and acting in
concert makes contributions to five or more candidates) and
Political Parties: $5,000 per candidate per special election and
$5,000 per special runoff election.  (Section 85305(c)(3).)


     5  In Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v.
Fair Political Practices Commission (9th Cir. 1992)  955 F.2d
1312, cert. denied, ___ U.S. ___, 112 S.Ct. 3056, a federal court
invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act
for elections other than federal elections.  (Sections 85301,
85302, and 85303; Lowell Advice Letter, I-91-064.)

     6  The Commission has determined that candidates may begin raising
contributions for a special election prior to the special election
cycle so long as the candidate has filed a statement of intention
to be a candidate for the elective office, pursuant to Section
85200, and the contributions accepted for the special election,
from any contributor, do not exceed the limits in Section 85305.
(Regulation 18535(a).)  However, contributions for a special
runoff election may only be solicited and received beginning the
day after the special election, where a special runoff election is
called.

Our File No. I-94-309
Page 5


     2.  Political Committee (a committee of persons that receives
contributions from two or more persons and acting in concert makes
contributions to candidates): $2,500 per candidate per special
election and $2,500 per special runoff election.  (Section
85305(c)(2).)

     3.  Person (basically everyone else--individual,
proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate,
business trust, company, corporation or association): $1,000 per
candidate per special election and $1,000 per special runoff
election.  (Section 85305(c)(1).)

     Please note, local contribution limits might also apply in
other elections not subject to the Act's limits, or may provide
lower contribution limits.  You should contact the city or county
clerk in the jurisdiction of the candidate to whom you wish to
contribute if you have questions regarding local limits.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division




7
     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

34th Assembly District Democratic Committee,
the committee is a
“recipient” committee.
a and b.
Recipient committees may raise funds and make
contributions to candidates for office.
2.
The Act does not prohibit the 34th Assembly District
Democratic Committee from making contributions to candidates.
However,
contribution limits do exist with respect to special
elections.
In addition,
local jurisdictions may limit
contributions.

Discretion as to whom to contribute to is left with the
34th Assembly District Democratic Committee.

requires that a committee contribute to all candidates in a given
election when the committee wishes to contribute to only select
committees in that election.
